title: Process Costing — Cost Flow Entries
event: In July, the Cutting Department transferred goods costing $140,000 to the Assembly Department, and Assembly completed goods costing $260,000. Prepare the transfer entries.

lines.label: transferred-in cost
lines.why: Costs follow the product — Assembly's WIP receives Cutting's finished work as 'transferred-in' cost.
lines.trap: Transferred-in is treated like a third cost category downstream, 100% complete on arrival.

lines.label: work leaves Cutting
lines.why: Cutting's WIP releases the cost of what it finished.
lines.trap: WIP to WIP — not to Finished Goods; the product isn't done, it just changed rooms.

entries.label: 2 — Completion out of the final department

lines.label: done goods
lines.why: Only the FINAL department transfers to Finished Goods.
lines.trap: Intermediate departments never touch Finished Goods — a wiring error exams test constantly.

lines.label: floor cleared
lines.why: Assembly releases the cost of completed production (its own costs plus transferred-in).
lines.trap: The 260,000 includes the 140,000 that arrived from Cutting — costs accumulate as they travel.

memorize.body: One WIP account PER department is the structural difference from job order costing. Costs cascade: Dept A → Dept B → Finished Goods → COGS.
Each transfer is a credit to the sender's WIP and a debit to the receiver — same pattern at every stage.

questions.prompt: Total credited to Work in Process—Assembly when goods are completed = ?

distractors.feedback: That's what Assembly RECEIVED from Cutting. What leaves for Finished Goods includes Assembly's own added costs: 260,000.

build.scaffold: Costs move with the product: credit the room it left, debit the room it entered.
